If not, you can just send me a message and I'll give it to you. But there are two main problems that freelancers have when working on retainer. The first is that they'll do an entire month's worth of work

## [06:03 - 09:05]

and then only send to the invoice after the month is over. So they will work for the whole month and then send the invoice for the month that just passed. The problem with that is if your client becomes insolvent or doesn't wanna work with you anymore or any number of things, you've done a full month's worth of work that they're not gonna pay you for and you have effectively no recourse, especially if they're bankrupt because even if you take them to court and win, if there's no money in that account, you're not getting paid. So the first challenge is not billing after the fact, but billing upfront. So the way I structure my retainers is the invoice goes out on the 15th and it covers the next month. So for example, it's December 2nd. So on December 15th, my clients are gonna get an invoice for my work in January and then they have until the end of December to pay me for my work in January. That way I'm always getting paid upfront and I'm never at risk of doing a month's worth of work and then not getting paid for the time that I've already spent. So that's the first piece. The second piece though is even if you do that, if you get to the first of the month, second of the month and you haven't been paid and then the client ghosts you, even though you're not gonna do any work without getting paid at that point, you're still, you've held space in your calendar, you've maybe turned down other clients to keep room and keep bandwidth for that retainer. So it's not fair to you for a client to just suddenly disappear and leave you with this massive hole in your calendar when you've been setting that time aside for them. So the other piece of this landlord retainer model, and I got the idea from Shady New York City landlords who make you pay a huge deposit up front to rent an apartment, it's the first month's rent and the last month's rent. So the same thing applies for this retainer approach is when you start the retainer, the client pays for their final month upfront. So that way, even if they decide last minute to cancel the project, you've been paid for a full month. So that way you've got time to transition to find another client, to replace them. And so not only are you protected from not working without being paid, which happens if you bill after the facts, you're also protected from the opportunity cost of setting time aside, of planning for that client's work to be coming next month, and then all of a sudden them dropping out of nowhere, at least you're protecting your cash flow, right? Cause if you're banking on that invoice for next month and all of a sudden it doesn't come, again, even if you're not working, even if you don't work without getting paid, you still lose that cash flow, it still causes other problems for your business. So better to get that last month upfront. So this is a, that's again, the landlord retainer in a nutshell. If you look in the main copy accelerator group,

All right, so the referral, I call this the 32nd referral method because with one 32nd conversation, you can get your clients to agree to give you referrals at the end of the project. So the way I came up with this was when I was freelancing on the side, between my day job, serving my clients and trying to find new clients, I just didn't have the time. So obviously I couldn't leave my day job and I couldn't stop serving my clients, otherwise they wouldn't pay me. So of those three, I had to figure out a way to streamline my client acquisition as much as possible while I was working on the side and keeping my day job. And what I came up with was figuring out a way to make my current clients bring me my next clients. So referrals are sort of the obvious solution to do that, but the mechanism of how do you actually make that happen in a way that is reliable was what I kind of cracked the code on how to do. And the high level philosophy is that your clients want as much of your time and as much of your attention as they can possibly get. They're paying you a good amount of money to write their copy. So every hour you spend out looking for your next client is an hour you're kind of stealing from your current clients. But at the same time, if you don't bring in new clients, you don't have a, your business is gonna go broke at some point. So you can't just totally give up client acquisition either. So it creates this sort of, what I would call an invisible conflict where your clients want more of your services or more of your attention, more of your time, but you can't give it to them without jeopardizing your own business, unless your clients will give you referrals because then you can stop going out and chasing new clients all the time. Instead, you can devote more of your time

## [18:08 - 21:08]

and your attention to your current clients during the project. And then in return, when the project is over, your clients can ask, you ask your clients to then give you two referrals to help you keep your own pipeline full. And so you're essentially trading more of your time and attention during the project for referrals after the project is over. And I have a whole script for how to sign, for exactly how to sort of phrase that and bring it up and propose it to your clients. So I will make a note of sharing that in the group too. So that has really been the key for me to getting all these referrals is because my clients will agree upfront to give me two referrals after the project is over in exchange for more of my time, more of my attention, more of my services during the project. And they love that, they're happy about that, right? Because their project gets done faster, they get more attention to detail, they get all these good things. And I'm happy about that, because I'm not gonna go waste a bunch of time hunting down new leads, they're just gonna show up in my inbox and their project is over. So it's a win all around.
